revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cs:Aoi Macuri]][[en:Aoi Matsuri]][[es:Aoi Matsuri]][[eu:Aoi Matsuri]][[fa:آئویی ماتسوری]][[fi:Aoi-matsuri]][[fr:Aoi matsuri]][[hu:Aoi macuri]][[id:Aoi Matsuri]][[ja:葵祭]][[ko:아오이마쓰리]][[pt:Aoi Matsuri]][[ru:Аой-мацури]][[simple:Aoi festival]][[tr:Aoi Festivali]][[uk:Аої мацурі]][[zh:葵祭]]
{{Short description|Traditional Japanese Festival in Kyoto|qq=}} {{Italic title|reason=|qq=}} [[File:Aoi_Matsuri.jpg|thumb|right|The ''Aoi Matsuri'' procession, departing in front of [[Kyoto Imperial Palace]]'s main gate ''Kenreimon'' (建礼門)]] [[File:Aoix2.jpg|thumb|right|Man carrying a hollyhock [[:en:Matsuri Float|float]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''Aoi Matsuri'''''|葵祭|qq=}}, or "[[:en:Hollyhock|Hollyhock]] Festival", (although commonly, but mistakenly identified as "hollyhock", the "aoi" actually belongs to the birthwort family and translates as "{{ill|Asarum|en|Asarum|qid=Q157718|lt=wild ginger|1=Asarum}}"—Asarum) is one of the three main annual [[Japanese festivals|festivals]] held in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, the other two being {{ill|Jidai Matsuri|en|Jidai Matsuri|qid=Q912124|lt=the Festival of the Ages|1=Jidai Matsuri}} (Jidai Matsuri) and the [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]]. It is a festival of the two Kamo shrines in the north of the city, [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and [[Kamigamo Shrine]]. The festival may also be referred to as the '''Kamo Festival'''. It is held on 15 May of each year. ==History== [[File:Saio dai Roto-nogi 20160515.JPG|thumb|left|The ''Saiō-Dai'' carried in her [[:en:Palanquin|Palanquin]]]] According to the ancient, presumed historical and regarded as accurate with some fantastic embellishments, record ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the festival originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|qid=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}} (reigned CE 539 - 571). The ancient records known as the ''Honchō getsurei'' ([[:jp:本朝月令|本朝月令]]) and ''Nenchūgyōji hissho'' (年中行事秘抄) reveal that a succession of disastrous rains with high winds ruined the grain crops, and epidemics had spread through the country. Because diviners placed the cause on divine punishment by the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo deities]], the Emperor sent his messenger with a retinue to the shrine to conduct various acts to appease the deities, in prayer for a bountiful harvest. These included riding a galloping horse.<ref name="kaiyu.omiki.com">{{Cite web|url=http://kaiyu.omiki.com/mioya/mioya.html|title=Mioya|website=kaiyu.omiki.com|qq=}}</ref> This became an annual ritual, and the galloping horse performance developed into an [[Yabusame|equestrian archery]] performance. According to the historical record known as the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' (続日本記, so many people had come to view this equestrian performance on the festival day in the 2nd year of the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|qid=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu|1=Emperor Monmu}} (r. 697–707) that the event was banned.<ref name="kaiyu.omiki.com" /> In the ninth century, [[Emperor Kanmu]] established the seat of the {{ill|Chrysanthemum Throne|en|Chrysanthemum Throne|qid=Q1981690|lt=imperial throne|1=Chrysanthemum Throne}} in Kyoto. This represented the beginning of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} in Japanese history. Emperor Kanmu recognized the [[kami|deities]] of the Kamo shrines as protectors of the Heian capital, and established the Aoi Matsuri as an annual imperial event.<ref name=aoi>Aoi, 2007</ref> The festival saw its peak of grandeur in the middleof the Heian period, but this waned in the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} and the following {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, and as the nation entered the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, the festival procession was discontinued. In the {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|qid=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} era (1688–1704) of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, it was revived, but in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} (1869), when the capital was moved from Kyoto to {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, observance of the festival procession stopped. In Meiji-17 (1885), it was again revived as part of a government plan to enliven Kyoto. All but the rituals at the shrine fronts were discontinued from 1944, due to the {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}. At last, the festival procession started to be held again from 1953. The ''Saiō-Dai'' festival princess tradition was initiated in 1956.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/koto/aoi/arekore/kigen.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20090417063352/http://kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/koto/aoi/arekore/kigen.html|url-status=dead|title=Kyoto Shimbun web page about the Aoi Festival (Japanese)|archive-date=April 17, 2009|qq=}}</ref> The ''[[Saiō]]'' (斎王) was an unmarried female member of the Japanese imperial house. The festival is named after the [[:en:Hollyhock|Hollyhock]] (''aoi'') leaves used as decoration throughout the celebration as well as offerings to the gods.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Japanese festivals|last=Bauer, Helen.|date=1974|publisher=Charles E. Tuttle|others=Carlquist, Sherwin John, 1930-|isbn=4-8053-0358-1|location=Tokyo|oclc=2165841|url-access=registration|url=https://archive.org/details/japanesefestival00hele|qq=}}</ref> During the Heian Period, these leaves were once believed to protect against natural disasters such as earthquakes and thunder, and were often hung under the roofs of homes for protection.<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|title=Japanese festivals|last=Haga, Hideo, 1921-|date=1986|publisher=Hoikusha|isbn=4-586-54013-3|edition=12th|location=Osaka, Japan|oclc=18304911|qq=}}</ref> The plants used in the Aoi Matsuri may not be hollyhock, but possibly {{ill|Asarum|en|Asarum|qid=Q157718|lt=wild ginger|1=Asarum}}. Although due to the rarity of these plants, other plants with similarly shaped leaves have been used in the festival instead, such as the leaves of the [[:en:katsura tree|katsura tree]].<ref name=":0" /> ==Festival events== [[File:Saio Dai and Women parade 2015.JPG|thumb|The ''Saiō-Dai'' and her retinue]] There are several events that take place during the month but the most important event is on 15 May. There are two parts to Aoi Matsuri: the procession (''rotō-no-gi'')<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Festivals of Japan : illustrated.|date=1985|publisher=Japan Travel Bureau|others=Nihon Kōtsū Kōsha.|isbn=4-533-00489-X|edition=1st|location=[Tokyo]|oclc=15628782|qq=}}</ref> and the shrine rites.<ref name="frang">Frang, 2002</ref> The procession is led by the Imperial Messenger. Following the imperial messenger are: two ox carts, four cows, thirty-six horses, and six hundred people.<ref name=frang/> The six hundred people are all wearing traditional dress of Heian nobles (''ōmiyabito''),<ref name=":1" /> while the oxcart (''gissha'') is adorned with artificial [[wisteria]] flowers.<ref name=":0" /> The procession starts at 10:30 on May 15 and leaves the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace]] in front of the main gate ''Kenreimon'' (建礼門) and slowly works its way towards the [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and finally the [[Kamigamo Shrine]].<ref name=shively>Shively, 1999</ref> When they finally arrive at both shrines, the ''Saiō-Dai'' and Imperial Messenger perform their rituals. The ''Saiō-Dai'' simply pays her respects to the deities and the Imperial Messenger intones the imperial rescript praising the deities and requesting their continued favor.<ref name=shively/> There are two main figures in the ''Aoi Matsuri'': the ''Saiō-Dai'' and the Imperial Messenger.<ref name=aoi/> Historically the ''Saiō-Dai'' was a woman of the imperial house who was chosen from the sisters and daughters of the emperor to dedicate herself to the Shimogamo shrine. The role of ''Saiō-Dai'' was to maintain ritual purity and to represent the emperor at the festival. Nowadays, the role of the ''Saiō-Dai'' is a {{ill|stand-in|en|stand-in|qid=Q2513186|lt=stand-in|1=Stand-in}} by an unmarried woman in Kyoto.<ref name=aoi/> She has to have the three attributes of wisdom, grace, and social standing. The selection process is strict and the news is announced in the press. It is the highest honour a woman can receive in Kyoto. The ''Saiō-Dai'' is dressed in the traditional style of the Heian court. Traditional Heian court dress for women would be wearing several layers of exquisitely colored silk robes.<ref>Layered, 1995</ref> The ''Saiō-Dai'' wears twelve layers of the traditional style robes ({{ill|Jūnihitoe|en|Jūnihitoe|qid=Q2418740|lt=Jūnihitoe|1=Jūnihitoe}}).<ref>(Shimogamo, 2009)</ref> To maintain [[misogi|ritual purity]], the ''Saiō-Dai'' goes through several ceremonies of purification before the procession of the festival. The Imperial Messenger leads the festival procession on horseback.<ref name=aoi/> During the Heian period he would be a Fifth-Rank courtier holding the office of middleor lesser captain and was usually a man destined for high office.<ref name=shively/> His role was to read the imperial rescript of the shrines and present the emperor’s offerings.<ref name=shively/> During the Heian period, the ''Saiō-Dai'' and the Imperial messenger would be accompanied by ten dancers and twelve musicians.<ref name=shively/> Also present during the procession are guards (''kebiishii),'' government officials, civic officials, military retainers, and a delegate from {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|qid=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro|1=Yamashiro Province}} (''Yamashiro-no-Tsukai)''.<ref name=":2" /> Also featured at the Aoi Matsuri are horse races (''kurabe-uma''),<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1015 ''"Kurabe-uma,"''] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto; n.b., this link incorporates streaming video of a horse race at [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].</ref> and demonstrations of {{ill|Mounted archery|en|Mounted archery|qid=Q1074665|lt=Mounted archery|1=Mounted archery}} ([[yabusame]]).<ref>[http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/raku/backnum/may07_e/buttone3.html ''"Aoi matsuri,"''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090604012219/http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/raku/backnum/may07_e/buttone3.html |date=2009-06-04 |qq=}} Kyoto City Tourism and Culture Information System.</ref> Many things in Kyoto are named after the Aoi. == See also == * ''{{ill|Saiō Matsuri|en|Saiō Matsuri|qid=Q7402591|lt=Saiō Matsuri|1=Saiō Matsuri}}'' in Ise, which is the traditional procession of the ''Saiō'' to Ise Shrine ==References== {{Reflist|qq=}} ==External links== * [http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/koto/aoi/aoi.html Kyoto Shimbun web site (Japanese only), with various video clips of the Aoi Festival.] ==Works cited== {{Commons Category|Aoi Matsuri|qq=}} * [http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/festivals/aoi.html Aoi Matsuri (2004). Retrieved from website on 20 Jan 2009] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110621061944/http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/traditionalevents/a24_fes_aoi.html Aoi Matsuri. (2009). Retrieved from website on 20 Jan 2009] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20090504063200/http://www.kyotoguide.com/ver2/thismonth/aoimatsuri.html Aoi Matsuri Festival. (2007). Retrieved from website on 20 Jan 2009] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20090531073520/http://www.asiarooms.com/travel-guide/japan/kyoto/kyoto-festivals-%26-events/aoi-matsuri-%28hollyhock-festival%29.html Aoi Matsuri (Hollyhock Festival). (2009). Retrieved from website on 20 January 2009] * Frang, M. (2002). Rites of Heritage. ''World & I.'' V.17(5): 176. Retrieved from MAS Ultra – School Edition database on 21 January 2009. * Layered Look, The. (1995) ''Civilization''. Mar/Apr. V.2(2): 22. Retrieved from Academic Search Premiere database on 20 January 2009. * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100701173957/http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/pg217.html Shimogamo Jinja. (2009). Retrieved from website on 21 Jan 2009] * [https://books.google.com/books?id=eiTWWfoyuyAC&dq=kamo+festival&pg=RA1-PA181 Shively, D., Hall, J., McCullough, W. (1999). The Cambridge History of Japan. Retrieved from Google Book Search on 20 Jan 2009] {{Coord missing|Kyoto Prefecture|qq=}}
